items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.correct: A recurring main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.).
items.misconception: A round where voices enter at different times.
items.why: Students confuse rondo (alternating refrain) with round (canon). Rondo: ABACA, ABACABA, etc. The A theme keeps coming back, like a refrain.

items.q: How do you identify a major key from its key signature?
items.correct: The last sharp is the leading note (semitone below tonic); the second-to-last flat is the tonic.
items.misconception: The number of sharps or flats equals the tonic note number.
items.why: Students lack a reliable rule. For sharp keys: last sharp + 1 semitone = tonic (e.g., F sharp means G major). For flat keys: the second-to-last flat IS the tonic (or memorise: 1 flat = F major).

items.q: A dot after a note adds?
items.why: It's A half of its value. “A quarter of its value” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many quavers equal a crotchet?
items.why: The real figure is 2 quavers. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
